---
title: "DCU Python Tutorials"
subtitle: "An interactive introduction to Python that runs in your browser"
author: "Damien Dupré, DCU Business School"
---

{{< include ./_extensions/r-wasm/live/_knitr.qmd >}}

Each grey box on this page is a real Python console. You can type in it, change the
code, and run it. The Python interpreter (a build called Pyodide) has been compiled
to run inside your web browser, so there is nothing to install, no account to
create, and no server to connect to.

A few things worth knowing before you start:

- Edit the code in a box, then press Run Code to see what it produces.
- Where you see `______` in the code, replace it with the missing piece. The
  comments next to it tell you what is expected.
- If you get stuck, use the Show Hints or Show Solution buttons under each exercise.

The first time you run a box, Python needs a few seconds to start up and download
the packages it uses (pandas and plotnine). That only happens once, so please be
patient.

## Getting started

Let us check that everything is working. Just press Run Code on the box below.

```{pyodide}
#| autorun: false
#| caption: Your first line of Python
# Anything after a '#' is a comment, so Python ignores it.
print("Hello World")
```

If `Hello World` appeared underneath, then Python is running in your browser and
you are ready to go.

# Chapter 1: Python basics

This chapter covers the pieces you will reach for again and again: variables,
lists, and dictionaries. You do not need any extra package here, since all of this
is plain Python.

## Variables and f-strings

A variable stores a value with `=` so that you can use it again later. An f-string,
which is text written as `f"..."`, lets you drop a variable straight into the text
by putting its name in curly braces. In each exercise below, replace every `______`
using the values described in the comments.

```{pyodide}
#| exercise: py_vars
# 1. Store your age (a number) and your name (text, in quotes)
my_age  = ______
my_name = "______"

# 2. Print a greeting that uses both variables
print(f"My name is {my_name} and I am {my_age} years old")

# 3. Make a list of numbers and count how many there are with len()
my_numbers = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]
print("Length:", ______(my_numbers))
```

::: { .hint exercise="py_vars" }
- Text needs quotes, like `"Damien"`, while a number does not, like `25`.
- `len(my_numbers)` returns how many items are in the list.
:::

```{pyodide}
#| exercise: py_vars
#| solution: true
my_age  = 25
my_name = "Damien"

print(f"My name is {my_name} and I am {my_age} years old")

my_numbers = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]
print("Length:", len(my_numbers))
```

## Lists and dictionaries

A list is an ordered collection written in square brackets `[ ]`, and you reach
into it by position. Python counts from 0, so the second item is `[1]`, not `[2]`.
A dictionary stores values under named keys, written in curly braces `{ }`.

```{pyodide}
#| exercise: py_lists
#| min-lines: 14
# 1. Make a list of the 4 provinces of Ireland
provinces = [______, ______, ______, ______]

# 2. Print the SECOND province (remember: indexing starts at 0!)
print(provinces[______])

# 3. Count the provinces with len(), and add the numbers with sum()
print("Number of provinces:", ______(provinces))

numbers = [10, 20, 30, 40, 50]
print("Sum:", ______(numbers))

# 4. Fill in this student's name and age, then print them
student = {"name": "______", "age": ______, "course": "Business Analytics"}
print(student["name"], "is", student["age"], "years old")
```

::: { .hint exercise="py_lists" }
- The four provinces are `"Connacht"`, `"Leinster"`, `"Munster"`, and `"Ulster"`.
- The second province is `provinces[1]`, not `provinces[2]`.
- `len()` counts the items, and `sum()` adds them up.
:::

```{pyodide}
#| exercise: py_lists
#| solution: true
provinces = ["Connacht", "Leinster", "Munster", "Ulster"]

print(provinces[1])

print("Number of provinces:", len(provinces))

numbers = [10, 20, 30, 40, 50]
print("Sum:", sum(numbers))

student = {"name": "Alice", "age": 21, "course": "Business Analytics"}
print(student["name"], "is", student["age"], "years old")
```

# Chapter 2: Data wrangling with pandas

Now for some real data. From here on we will use the gapminder dataset, which
records life expectancy, population, and GDP per capita for most countries between
1952 and 2007. We read it straight from the web with pandas, the standard Python
library for working with tables (which pandas calls DataFrames).

> A quick note on Polars and pandas. The classroom version of this course uses
> Polars. Polars is written in Rust and has no WebAssembly build, so it cannot run
> inside the browser. For this in-browser edition we use pandas instead, which
> Pyodide includes out of the box. The ideas are the same in both: filter rows,
> pick columns, and group.

Run the box below to load the data and take a first look.

```{pyodide}
#| autorun: false
#| caption: Load the gapminder data from the web
import pandas as pd

gapminder = pd.read_csv(
    "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/damien-dupre/data/refs/heads/master/gapminder.csv"
)
gapminder.head()
```

The columns are `country`, `continent`, `year`, `lifeExp`, `pop`, and `gdpPercap`.

## Filter rows and select columns

You filter rows with a condition inside square brackets, and you select columns by
passing a list of their names.

```{pyodide}
#| exercise: py_filter
#| setup: true
import pandas as pd
gapminder = pd.read_csv("https://raw.githubusercontent.com/damien-dupre/data/refs/heads/master/gapminder.csv")
```

```{pyodide}
#| exercise: py_filter
#| min-lines: 6
# 1. Keep only the rows for the year 2007 where population is over 100 million
gapminder[(gapminder["year"] == ______) & (gapminder["pop"] > ______)]

# 2. Keep only the country and lifeExp columns
gapminder[[______, ______]]
```

::: { .hint exercise="py_filter" }
- One hundred million is written `100000000`, with no commas in Python.
- To choose columns you pass their names as strings in a list, like `["country", "lifeExp"]`.
:::

```{pyodide}
#| exercise: py_filter
#| solution: true
gapminder[(gapminder["year"] == 2007) & (gapminder["pop"] > 100000000)]

gapminder[["country", "lifeExp"]]
```

## Group and summarise

`groupby()` splits the table into groups. You then pick a column and apply a
summary such as `.mean()`, which answers questions like "what is the average life
expectancy on each continent?".

```{pyodide}
#| exercise: py_groupby
#| setup: true
import pandas as pd
gapminder = pd.read_csv("https://raw.githubusercontent.com/damien-dupre/data/refs/heads/master/gapminder.csv")
```

```{pyodide}
#| exercise: py_groupby
# Mean life expectancy per continent, for the year 2007:
(
    gapminder[gapminder["year"] == ______]
    .groupby("______")["______"]
    .mean()
)
```

::: { .hint exercise="py_groupby" }
- Filter to `year == 2007`, group by `"continent"`, then take the `.mean()` of `"lifeExp"`.
:::

```{pyodide}
#| exercise: py_groupby
#| solution: true
(
    gapminder[gapminder["year"] == 2007]
    .groupby("continent")["lifeExp"]
    .mean()
)
```

# Chapter 3: Visualisation with plotnine

`plotnine` brings the grammar of graphics to Python, which is the same approach as
ggplot2 in R. You start from the data, map variables to things you can see (the x
position, the y position, the colour, and so on) with `aes()`, and then add a
geometry such as `geom_point()` to draw them.

```{pyodide}
#| exercise: py_plot
#| setup: true
import pandas as pd
gapminder = pd.read_csv("https://raw.githubusercontent.com/damien-dupre/data/refs/heads/master/gapminder.csv")
```

```{pyodide}
#| exercise: py_plot
#| min-lines: 12
# Recreate the chart Hans Rosling made well known, for the year 2007:
# wealth (gdpPercap, on a log scale) against health (lifeExp),
# with one colour per continent.
from plotnine import ggplot, aes, geom_point, scale_x_log10, labs, theme_minimal

df = gapminder[gapminder["year"] == 2007]

(
    ggplot(df, aes(x="______", y="______", color="______"))
    + geom_point(size=2, alpha=0.7)
    + scale_x_log10()
    + labs(
        title="Wealth and health across the world, 2007",
        x="GDP per capita (log scale)",
        y="Life expectancy (years)",
        color="Continent",
    )
    + theme_minimal()
)
```

::: { .hint exercise="py_plot" }
- Map `"gdpPercap"` to `x`, `"lifeExp"` to `y`, and `"continent"` to `color`.
- In plotnine the column names go in quotes inside `aes()`.
:::

```{pyodide}
#| exercise: py_plot
#| solution: true
from plotnine import ggplot, aes, geom_point, scale_x_log10, labs, theme_minimal

df = gapminder[gapminder["year"] == 2007]

(
    ggplot(df, aes(x="gdpPercap", y="lifeExp", color="continent"))
    + geom_point(size=2, alpha=0.7)
    + scale_x_log10()
    + labs(
        title="Wealth and health across the world, 2007",
        x="GDP per capita (log scale)",
        y="Life expectancy (years)",
        color="Continent",
    )
    + theme_minimal()
)
```

# Where to go next

You have just filtered, summarised, and plotted real data without installing
anything on your machine. There is of course a lot more to Python than this, and
the only way to get comfortable with it is to keep writing and running code.

If you would like the longer version of this course, the original one (which runs
on a server) is at
[dcu-python-tutorials](https://damien-dupre.github.io/dcu-python-tutorials/). If
you prefer R, the same ideas are in the
[R live tutorials](https://damien-dupre.github.io/dcu-r-tutorials-live/).

One last thing: every box on this page keeps your edits for as long as the tab is
open, so do feel free to experiment. If you want the original code back, press
Start Over on the exercise.
